expanded_description:
The LM curve (Liquidity preference-Money supply curve) represents all combinations of the real interest rate and output (GDP) at which the money market is in equilibrium—that is, where real money demand equals real money supply. The LM curve is derived from the liquidity preference theory of money demand and shows the positive relationship between the interest rate and output: higher output increases money demand (transactions motive), which raises the interest rate (given a fixed money supply); lower output decreases money demand, lowering the interest rate. This positive slope is the defining characteristic of the LM curve.

Understanding the LM curve is essential for analyzing how monetary policy affects interest rates and output, for determining equilibrium in the money market, and for combining with the IS curve to find general equilibrium. The LM curve shifts when factors other than output change: changes in the money supply (monetary policy) or changes in the price level shift the LM curve. An increase in the money supply shifts the LM curve right (lower interest rate for any given output); a decrease shifts it left. Changes in money demand (e.g., financial innovation, payment technology) also shift the LM curve.

In this section, we will derive the LM curve from the money market equilibrium condition, analyze the slope and determinants of the LM curve, examine the factors that shift the LM curve, explore the liquidity trap (where the LM curve becomes horizontal), and discuss the classical case (where the LM curve becomes vertical). You will learn to calculate the slope of the LM curve, predict the effects of monetary policy on interest rates and output, and understand how the LM curve interacts with the IS curve to determine short-run equilibrium.

core_concept.principle:
The LM curve (Liquidity preference-Money supply curve) shows the positive relationship between the real interest rate ($r$) and output ($Y$) in the money market. It is derived from the equilibrium condition that real money demand equals real money supply.

Money Market Equilibrium Condition:
$\frac{M}{P} = L(Y, r)$
where:
• $M$ = nominal money supply (exogenous, controlled by central bank)
• $P$ = price level (fixed in short run)
• $L(Y, r)$ = real money demand, increasing in $Y$ (transactions motive) and decreasing in $r$ (opportunity cost of holding money)

Key Assumptions:
• Money demand function: $L(Y, r) = kY - h r$, where $k > 0$ is the income sensitivity of money demand, and $h > 0$ is the interest sensitivity of money demand.
• Money supply $M$ is exogenous (controlled by central bank).
• Price level $P$ is fixed (short-run assumption).

Derivation of the LM Curve:
1. Start with $\frac{M}{P} = kY - h r$.
2. Solve for $r$ in terms of $Y$ (or $Y$ in terms of $r$).

$kY - h r = \frac{M}{P}$
$h r = kY - \frac{M}{P}$
$r = \frac{k}{h} Y - \frac{1}{h} \frac{M}{P}$

This is the equation of the LM curve. The slope with respect to $Y$ is $\frac{dr}{dY} = \frac{k}{h} > 0$.

Slope of the LM Curve:
• Steeper LM curve: Money demand is very sensitive to income ($k$ large) OR money demand is insensitive to interest rates ($h$ small).
• Flatter LM curve: Money demand is insensitive to income ($k$ small) OR money demand is very sensitive to interest rates ($h$ large).

Shifts of the LM Curve:
• Rightward shift (expansionary monetary policy): $M \uparrow$ (money supply increase) → LM shifts right (lower $r$ for any given $Y$).
• Leftward shift (contractionary monetary policy): $M \downarrow$ → LM shifts left (higher $r$ for any given $Y$).
• Also shifts from changes in $P$ (price level) or changes in money demand ($k$ or $h$).

Special Cases:
• Liquidity trap (horizontal LM): When interest rates are very low, money demand becomes perfectly elastic ($h \rightarrow \infty$). The LM curve becomes horizontal. Monetary policy is ineffective (cannot lower rates further).
• Classical case (vertical LM): If money demand is insensitive to interest rates ($h = 0$), the LM curve is vertical. Changes in money supply affect prices, not output (monetary neutrality).
core_concept.explanation:
Imagine the money market as a seesaw. On one side is the real money supply ($M/P$), controlled by the central bank. On the other side is real money demand, which depends on output (income) and the interest rate. When output is high, people need more money for transactions, shifting money demand right, which raises the interest rate. When output is low, money demand falls, lowering the interest rate. This is why the LM curve slopes upward.

Now consider the slope of the LM curve. If money demand is very sensitive to income ($k$ is large), a small increase in output causes a large increase in money demand, requiring a large increase in the interest rate to choke off the excess demand. The LM curve is steep. If money demand is very sensitive to interest rates ($h$ is large), a small increase in interest rates causes a large decrease in money demand, so only a small interest rate increase is needed to restore equilibrium. The LM curve is flat.

At the zero lower bound, interest rates cannot fall below zero. When rates are already near zero, money demand becomes perfectly elastic (people will hold any amount of money at that rate). The LM curve becomes horizontal. This is the liquidity trap: monetary policy is ineffective because increasing the money supply does not lower interest rates further.

In the classical case (money demand insensitive to interest rates, $h = 0$), the LM curve is vertical. Changes in the money supply affect the price level, not output. This is the classical dichotomy: money is neutral.

The LM curve is only half of the IS-LM model. It tells us what interest rate will clear the money market for a given output. The IS curve tells us what output clears the goods market for a given interest rate. Their intersection gives the short-run equilibrium of the economy.

image.alt: Graph: LM curve upward sloping (r on y-axis, Y on x-axis). Points above the LM curve (excess supply of money) and below (excess demand for money).
image.caption: Figure 9.3.1: The LM curve shows combinations of interest rate and output where the money market is in equilibrium. It slopes upward because higher output increases money demand, raising the interest rate.

sections.introduction:
The LM curve (Liquidity preference-Money supply curve) represents all combinations of the real interest rate and output (GDP) at which the money market is in equilibrium—that is, where real money demand equals real money supply. The LM curve is derived from the liquidity preference theory of money demand and shows the positive relationship between the interest rate and output: higher output increases money demand (transactions motive), which raises the interest rate (given a fixed money supply); lower output decreases money demand, lowering the interest rate. This positive slope is the defining characteristic of the LM curve.

Understanding the LM curve is essential for analyzing how monetary policy affects interest rates and output, for determining equilibrium in the money market, and for combining with the IS curve to find general equilibrium. The LM curve shifts when factors other than output change: changes in the money supply (monetary policy) or changes in the price level shift the LM curve. An increase in the money supply shifts the LM curve right (lower interest rate for any given output); a decrease shifts it left. Changes in money demand (e.g., financial innovation, payment technology) also shift the LM curve.

In this section, we will derive the LM curve from the money market equilibrium condition, analyze the slope and determinants of the LM curve, examine the factors that shift the LM curve, explore the liquidity trap (where the LM curve becomes horizontal), and discuss the classical case (where the LM curve becomes vertical).

introduction_image.alt: Graph: Money market with vertical money supply and downward-sloping money demand; equilibrium interest rate at intersection.
introduction_image.caption: Figure 9.3.2: The LM curve is derived from the money market. For a given output, money demand is determined; equilibrium interest rate is found where money demand equals money supply.

key_concept.definition:
LM Curve: The set of all combinations of the real interest rate ($r$) and output ($Y$) for which the money market is in equilibrium (real money demand equals real money supply).

Money Market Equilibrium Condition:
$\frac{M}{P} = L(Y, r)$

Money Demand Function:
$L(Y, r) = kY - h r$
where:
• $k > 0$ = income sensitivity of money demand (how much money demand increases when output rises).
• $h > 0$ = interest sensitivity of money demand (how much money demand falls when interest rates rise).

Derivation:
$\frac{M}{P} = kY - h r$
$h r = kY - \frac{M}{P}$
$r = \frac{k}{h} Y - \frac{1}{h} \frac{M}{P}$

This is the equation of the LM curve. The slope is $\frac{dr}{dY} = \frac{k}{h} > 0$.

Slope Determinants:
• Steep LM: $k$ large (money demand very sensitive to income) OR $h$ small (money demand insensitive to interest rates).
• Flat LM: $k$ small (money demand insensitive to income) OR $h$ large (money demand very sensitive to interest rates).

Shifts of the LM Curve:
• Rightward shift (expansionary monetary policy): $M \uparrow$ → LM shifts down/right (lower $r$ for each $Y$).
• Leftward shift (contractionary monetary policy): $M \downarrow$ → LM shifts up/left (higher $r$ for each $Y$).
• Price level change: $P \uparrow$ reduces real money supply → LM shifts left (higher $r$).
• Money demand shock (change in $k$ or $h$): e.g., financial innovation reduces $k$ → LM shifts right.

Special Cases:
• Liquidity trap (horizontal LM): When $r$ is near zero, money demand becomes perfectly elastic ($h \rightarrow \infty$). LM curve is horizontal. Monetary policy ineffective.
• Classical case (vertical LM): If $h = 0$ (money demand insensitive to interest rates), LM curve is vertical. Changes in $M$ affect prices ($P$), not output ($Y$).
key_concept.context: The LM curve was introduced by John Hicks (1937) as part of the IS-LM model. The 'LM' stands for Liquidity preference-Money supply. It formalizes Keynes's concept of liquidity preference: the demand for money as a store of value (speculative motive) as well as a medium of exchange (transactions motive). The interest rate is the price that equilibrates the demand and supply for money. The LM curve was the centerpiece of the monetarist critique of Keynesian economics: monetarists argued that money demand is stable and interest-insensitive (vertical LM), so monetary policy is powerful and fiscal policy is ineffective. Keynesians argued that money demand is interest-sensitive (flat LM), so monetary policy is less effective and fiscal policy is more effective. The slope of the LM curve remains an empirical question; most estimates suggest it is upward sloping but not vertical or horizontal in normal times. At the zero lower bound (2008-2015, 2020-2022), the LM curve became effectively horizontal (liquidity trap).

image.alt: Two panels: Left - Money market for Y1 and Y2 (Y2 > Y1 gives higher money demand, higher equilibrium r). Right - LM curve plotting (Y1, r1) and (Y2, r2).
image.caption: Figure 9.3.3: Derivation of the LM curve from the money market. Higher output increases money demand, raising the equilibrium interest rate.

classifications.type: Slope of the LM Curve: Income Sensitivity vs. Interest Sensitivity
classifications.value: The LM curve slopes upward; its steepness depends on the income sensitivity of money demand ($k$) and the interest sensitivity of money demand ($h$)
characteristics: Steep LM: $k$ large (money demand very responsive to income) OR $h$ small (money demand unresponsive to interest rates).
Flat LM: $k$ small (money demand unresponsive to income) OR $h$ large (money demand very responsive to interest rates).
Vertical LM (classical case): $h = 0$ (money demand does not respond to interest rates).
Horizontal LM (liquidity trap): $h \rightarrow \infty$ (money demand infinitely elastic at very low interest rates).
classifications.behavior:
The slope of the LM curve determines how much the interest rate changes when output changes. If the LM curve is steep (large $k$ or small $h$), a small increase in output causes a large increase in the interest rate. This is because money demand rises sharply with income ($k$ large) and/or the interest rate must rise a lot to reduce money demand ($h$ small).

If the LM curve is flat (small $k$ or large $h$), a large increase in output causes only a small increase in the interest rate. Money demand does not rise much with income ($k$ small) and/or a small interest rate increase reduces money demand a lot ($h$ large).

The slope of the LM curve has important implications for policy effectiveness:
• Flat LM (Keynesian case): Monetary policy is less effective (because changes in $M$ have small effects on $r$), but fiscal policy is more effective (because there is little crowding out; $r$ does not rise much).
• Steep LM (Monetarist case): Monetary policy is more effective (changes in $M$ have large effects on $r$), but fiscal policy is less effective (crowding out is strong; $r$ rises a lot).
• Vertical LM (classical case): Monetary policy is very effective (changes in $M$ change $r$ and output), but fiscal policy is completely ineffective (crowding out is 100%). Actually, in the classical case, changes in $M$ change $P$, not $Y$, in the long run—but in the short run with fixed prices, a vertical LM means fiscal policy has no effect because any increase in $G$ is fully crowded out by higher $r$.
• Horizontal LM (liquidity trap): Monetary policy is completely ineffective (changes in $M$ do not change $r$), but fiscal policy is very effective (no crowding out).

image.alt: Three LM curves: steep (large k, small h), moderate, flat (small k, large h).
image.caption: Figure 9.3.4: The slope of the LM curve depends on the income sensitivity ($k$) and interest sensitivity ($h$) of money demand.

examples.process: Steep LM Curve (h small, k large)
examples.explanation: If $k = 0.5$ and $h = 0.1$, then slope $dr/dY = 0.5/0.1 = 5$. A $\$100$ billion increase in output raises the interest rate by 0.5 percentage points (500 basis points? Wait: $dr = 5 \times dY$; if $dY = 0.1$ trillion, $dr = 0.5$ = 50 basis points). Actually careful: If $Y$ is in trillions, $dr/dY = 5$ means a $\$1$ trillion increase in $Y$ raises $r$ by 5 percentage points. This is a very steep LM curve (monetarist view).

examples.process: Flat LM Curve (h large, k small)
examples.explanation: If $k = 0.2$ and $h = 1.0$, then slope $dr/dY = 0.2/1.0 = 0.2$. A $\$1$ trillion increase in $Y$ raises $r$ by only 0.2 percentage points (20 basis points). This is a flat LM curve (Keynesian view).

classifications.type: Shifts of the LM Curve: Monetary Policy and Price Level Changes
classifications.value: Changes in the money supply or price level shift the LM curve horizontally or vertically
characteristics: Increase in money supply ($M \uparrow$): LM shifts right/down (lower $r$ for each $Y$).
Decrease in money supply ($M \downarrow$): LM shifts left/up (higher $r$ for each $Y$).
Increase in price level ($P \uparrow$): reduces real money supply → LM shifts left/up.
Decrease in price level ($P \downarrow$): increases real money supply → LM shifts right/down.
Money demand shocks (change in $k$ or $h$): e.g., financial innovation reduces $k$ → LM shifts right.
The shift is vertical in $(Y, r)$ space: for a given $Y$, $r$ changes by $\Delta r = -\frac{1}{h} \Delta (M/P)$.
classifications.behavior:
The LM curve shifts when any factor other than output changes that affects the money market. The most important factor is monetary policy: when the central bank increases the money supply, the real money supply ($M/P$) increases. At any given output, there is now an excess supply of money. People try to get rid of the extra money by buying bonds, which drives bond prices up and interest rates down. The LM curve shifts down (or right).

The size of the shift depends on the interest sensitivity of money demand ($h$). If $h$ is large (money demand is very sensitive to interest rates), a small decrease in interest rates will absorb the excess money supply, so the LM curve shifts only a little. If $h$ is small (money demand is insensitive to interest rates), interest rates must fall a lot to absorb the excess money supply, so the LM curve shifts a lot.

Changes in the price level also shift the LM curve. If prices rise ($P \uparrow$), the real money supply falls, shifting the LM curve left (higher interest rates at any output). This is why inflation can tighten monetary conditions even if the central bank does not act.

Financial innovation (e.g., ATMs, credit cards, mobile payments) reduces the demand for money ($k$ falls). This shifts the LM curve right (lower interest rates at any output), all else equal. This is one reason interest rates have trended downward over the past 40 years.

image.alt: Graph: LM curve shifting right (increase in M or decrease in P) and left (decrease in M or increase in P).
image.caption: Figure 9.3.5: Monetary policy shifts the LM curve. An increase in the money supply shifts the LM curve right (lower interest rates at any given output).

examples.explanation: In March 2020, the Fed increased the money supply dramatically through QE. The LM curve shifted right. At any given output, interest rates fell. The federal funds rate dropped from 1.5% to 0.25% (though the ZLB limited further drops). The shift was large but the LM curve was already flat (liquidity trap).

In the 1970s, high inflation eroded the real money supply ($M/P$ fell). The LM curve shifted left, raising interest rates. This contributed to the high interest rates of the Volcker era (though Volcker also actively reduced $M$).

classifications.type: Special Cases: Liquidity Trap and Classical Case
classifications.value: At very low interest rates, the LM curve becomes horizontal (liquidity trap); if money demand is interest-insensitive, the LM curve is vertical (classical case)
characteristics: Liquidity trap: $h \rightarrow \infty$ (money demand perfectly elastic). LM curve horizontal at $r = r_{min}$ (usually zero).
In liquidity trap: Monetary policy ineffective (changes in $M$ do not change $r$). Fiscal policy very effective (no crowding out).
Classical case: $h = 0$ (money demand insensitive to interest rates). LM curve vertical.
Classical case (vertical LM): Changes in $M$ affect $P$, not $Y$, in the long run. In short run with fixed $P$, changes in $M$ affect $Y$ but fiscal policy is ineffective (full crowding out).
These extreme cases help explain the effectiveness of policy under different conditions.
classifications.behavior:
The liquidity trap occurs when the nominal interest rate is at or near zero. At this point, the opportunity cost of holding money is zero, so people are willing to hold any amount of money. Money demand becomes perfectly elastic ($h \rightarrow \infty$). The LM curve becomes horizontal. In a liquidity trap, increasing the money supply does not lower interest rates further (they are already at zero), so conventional monetary policy is ineffective. This is what happened in the US from 2008-2015 and 2020-2022 (though the 2020-2022 period eventually saw inflation, breaking the trap). Fiscal policy becomes very effective because there is no crowding out (interest rates cannot rise).

The classical case occurs when money demand is completely insensitive to interest rates ($h = 0$). This might happen if people hold money only for transactions and the opportunity cost is irrelevant (e.g., if interest rates are very low or if there are no alternative assets). In this case, the LM curve is vertical. Changes in the money supply have no effect on interest rates (the money demand curve is vertical, so the intersection with the vertical supply curve determines $r$? Actually careful: if $h=0$, $L = kY$, so $M/P = kY$. Then $Y = (1/k)(M/P)$. Output is determined solely by money supply and prices, not by interest rates. The LM curve is vertical at that $Y$. In this case, monetary policy is very powerful (it directly determines $Y$), and fiscal policy is ineffective because any increase in $G$ would require an increase in $Y$, but $Y$ is fixed by money supply; instead, interest rates rise to crowd out investment completely. This is the 'monetarist' view of the world.

image.alt: Two graphs: Horizontal LM (liquidity trap) and vertical LM (classical case).
image.caption: Figure 9.3.6: Special cases of the LM curve: horizontal (liquidity trap, monetary policy ineffective) and vertical (classical case, fiscal policy ineffective).

examples.explanation: Japan's policy rate hit zero in the 1990s and stayed near zero for decades. The BOJ engaged in massive QE, but the LM curve was horizontal; interest rates did not fall further. Monetary policy was ineffective. Fiscal policy (government spending) was needed to stimulate the economy, but Japan was slow to use it.

In the long run, money demand may become interest-insensitive as inflation expectations adjust. The LM curve is effectively vertical. A permanent increase in the money supply leads to a proportional increase in prices, not output. This is the classical dichotomy. The short-run IS-LM model with fixed prices does not capture this; the AD-AS model does.

principles.principle: The LM Curve Slopes Upward Because Higher Output Increases Money Demand, Raising Interest Rates
principles.statement: The positive slope of the LM curve is a fundamental implication of liquidity preference theory. When output rises, households and firms need more money to conduct transactions (transactions motive). With a fixed money supply, the increased demand for money creates an excess demand in the money market. People sell bonds to obtain more money, driving bond prices down and interest rates up. The higher interest rate reduces the quantity of money demanded (opportunity cost effect), restoring equilibrium. Thus, higher output is associated with higher interest rates. This relationship implies that monetary policy (which shifts the LM curve) can affect the economy, and that fiscal policy (which shifts the IS curve) will also affect interest rates (crowding out). The slope of the LM curve determines the extent of crowding out: a steep LM curve means crowding out is strong; a flat LM curve means crowding out is weak.
principles.application: Policymakers should consider the slope of the LM curve when designing policy. If the LM curve is flat (Keynesian view, low interest rates), fiscal policy is more effective because interest rates do not rise much (crowding out is minimal). If the LM curve is steep (monetarist view, high interest rates or interest-insensitive money demand), monetary policy is more effective, and fiscal policy may be ineffective due to crowding out. In a liquidity trap (horizontal LM), monetary policy is useless, and fiscal policy is the only option.
principles.example: In 2009-2015, the US was in a liquidity trap (LM horizontal). The Fed's QE did not lower interest rates (they were already near zero), but it did provide liquidity. Fiscal policy (ARRA) was effective because there was no crowding out. In contrast, in 1994, the Fed raised rates preemptively; the LM curve was steep (normal conditions), and fiscal policy would have been less effective (higher rates would have crowded out investment).

image.alt: Graph: Flat LM (fiscal policy effective), steep LM (monetary policy effective).
image.caption: Figure 9.3.7: The slope of the LM curve determines the effectiveness of monetary vs. fiscal policy.

principles.principle: Monetary Policy Shifts the LM Curve; the Size of the Shift Depends on the Interest Sensitivity of Money Demand
principles.statement: When the central bank increases the money supply, the LM curve shifts right (down). For any given level of output, the interest rate is lower because there is now an excess supply of money. The magnitude of the shift depends on the interest sensitivity of money demand ($h$). If $h$ is large (money demand very sensitive to interest rates), a small decrease in interest rates will absorb the excess money supply, so the LM curve shifts only a little. If $h$ is small (money demand insensitive to interest rates), interest rates must fall a lot to absorb the excess money supply, so the LM curve shifts a lot. This is why monetary policy is more powerful (larger effects on interest rates and output) when money demand is interest-insensitive. Conversely, when money demand is highly interest-sensitive (flat LM), monetary policy has small effects on interest rates and output.
principles.application: Central banks should calibrate their policy actions based on estimates of $h$. If money demand is very interest-sensitive (as in a liquidity trap), increasing the money supply will have little effect on interest rates; the central bank must use unconventional tools (QE, forward guidance). If money demand is interest-insensitive (as in high-inflation environments), small changes in the money supply can have large effects on interest rates and output; the central bank must be cautious to avoid over-tightening.
principles.example: In 2008-2015, money demand was highly interest-sensitive (liquidity trap). The Fed increased the money supply dramatically (QE), but interest rates barely changed (already near zero). The LM curve shifted right, but the shift was horizontal—no effect on interest rates. In the 1980s, money demand was less interest-sensitive (Volcker disinflation). The Fed reduced the money supply, and interest rates rose sharply (to 20%), shifting the LM curve left significantly.

image.alt: Graph: LM shift right (M↑); size of shift depends on h (flat LM: small shift; steep LM: large shift).
image.caption: Figure 9.3.8: The effect of monetary policy on the LM curve depends on the interest sensitivity of money demand ($h$).

worked_examples.title: Deriving the LM Curve from Money Market Equilibrium

image.alt: Table: M=1000, P=2, so M/P=500. Money demand: L = 0.5Y - 20r. Derivation: 500 = 0.5Y - 20r → 20r = 0.5Y - 500 → r = 0.025Y - 25.
image.caption: Figure 9.3.9: Worked example: deriving the LM curve equation from the money market equilibrium condition.

worked_examples.problem:
Consider an economy with the following money market characteristics:

• Money supply: $M = 1000$
• Price level: $P = 2$ (fixed in short run)
• Money demand: $L(Y, r) = 0.5Y - 20r$ (where $r$ is the interest rate in percent)

a) Calculate the real money supply ($M/P$).
b) Derive the equation for the LM curve ($r$ as a function of $Y$).
c) Calculate the slope of the LM curve ($dr/dY$).
d) Calculate the interest rate when output is $Y = 2000$.
e) If the central bank increases the money supply to $M = 1200$, derive the new LM curve and calculate the new interest rate at $Y = 2000$.

step_by_step.explanation:
Set real money supply equal to real money demand:

$500 = 0.5Y - 20r$
$20r = 0.5Y - 500$
$r = \frac{0.5Y - 500}{20} = 0.025Y - 25$

LM curve: $r = 0.025Y - 25$

From $r = 0.025Y - 25$, the slope is $\frac{dr}{dY} = 0.025$ (or 2.5 basis points per unit of Y).

If $Y$ is in billions, a $\$1$ billion increase in $Y$ raises $r$ by 0.025 percentage points (2.5 basis points).

$r = 0.025(2000) - 25 = 50 - 25 = 25\%$

This is a very high interest rate, but the numbers are illustrative.

step_by_step.step: Derive new LM curve with M = 1200
step_by_step.explanation:
New real money supply = $1200 / 2 = 600$

$600 = 0.5Y - 20r$
$20r = 0.5Y - 600$
$r = \frac{0.5Y - 600}{20} = 0.025Y - 30$

New LM curve: $r = 0.025Y - 30$

At $Y = 2000$, new $r = 0.025(2000) - 30 = 50 - 30 = 20\%$

The increase in money supply lowered the interest rate from 25% to 20% (a 5 percentage point decrease).

worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying the money market equilibrium condition to derive the LM curve, calculate the slope, and determine the effects of monetary policy.

worked_examples.title: Analyzing the Liquidity Trap and Monetary Policy Effectiveness

image.alt: Graph: Horizontal LM at r=0; money supply increase shifts LM right but r unchanged at 0.
image.caption: Figure 9.3.10: Worked example: liquidity trap where the LM curve is horizontal and monetary policy is ineffective.

worked_examples.problem:
An economy is in a liquidity trap. The money demand function is $L(Y, r) = kY - h r$, but at $r = 0\%$, money demand becomes perfectly elastic ($h \rightarrow \infty$). Initially, $M/P = 500$, $k = 0.5$, and the LM curve is $r = 0.025Y - 25$ for $r > 0$, but at $r = 0$, the LM curve is horizontal.

a) What is the maximum output that can be achieved before the interest rate becomes positive (the point where the LM curve leaves the horizontal segment)?
b) If the central bank increases the money supply by 20% (to $M/P = 600$), what happens to the interest rate and output in the liquidity trap?
c) If the central bank increases the money supply further to $M/P = 1000$, what happens?
d) Explain why monetary policy is ineffective in a liquidity trap.
e) What policy would be effective in a liquidity trap?

step_by_step.step: Find the output at which the LM curve leaves the horizontal segment
step_by_step.explanation:
The LM curve is horizontal at $r = 0$ for low output levels. The upward-sloping part starts when the equation $r = 0.025Y - 25$ yields $r = 0$.

Set $0 = 0.025Y - 25$ → $0.025Y = 25$ → $Y = 25 / 0.025 = 1000$

For $Y \leq 1000$, the LM curve is horizontal at $r = 0$. For $Y > 1000$, the LM curve slopes upward with $r = 0.025Y - 25$.

The new LM curve equation (for $r > 0$) is $r = 0.025Y - 30$ (from the previous example). The horizontal segment still exists at $r = 0$, but the threshold output changes.

Find new threshold: $0 = 0.025Y - 30$ → $0.025Y = 30$ → $Y = 30 / 0.025 = 1200$

If the economy is initially at $Y < 1000$ (in the liquidity trap), the increase in money supply shifts the threshold to 1200, but the interest rate remains at $0$ and output does not increase unless the economy was at the threshold. In the liquidity trap, the LM curve is horizontal at $r=0$ for all $Y$ up to the threshold. Increasing $M$ does not lower $r$ (it's already zero) and does not increase $Y$ (the IS curve determines $Y$).

Monetary policy is ineffective: interest rate remains at 0%, output unchanged.

step_by_step.step: Effect of larger money supply increase (M/P = 1000)
step_by_step.explanation:
New real money supply = 1000. New LM equation (for $r>0$): $1000 = 0.5Y - 20r$ → $20r = 0.5Y - 1000$ → $r = 0.025Y - 50$

Threshold: $0 = 0.025Y - 50$ → $Y = 2000$

Even with a very large money supply increase, the interest rate remains at 0% for all $Y \leq 2000$. If the IS curve intersects the LM curve in the horizontal segment, output is determined by the IS curve, not by money supply. Monetary policy still cannot increase output unless the IS curve shifts right (fiscal policy).

Only when the money supply increase is so large that the threshold exceeds the IS-determined output will monetary policy affect interest rates (and then output). But in a deep recession, the IS curve may be far left, requiring enormous money supply increases to escape the trap—and even then, the zero lower bound prevents negative rates.

step_by_step.step: Explain why monetary policy is ineffective
step_by_step.explanation:
In a liquidity trap, the nominal interest rate is at zero. The central bank cannot lower it further (cannot go negative in a meaningful way). Money demand becomes perfectly elastic: people are willing to hold any amount of money at zero interest. Increasing the money supply simply adds to idle cash balances; it does not stimulate spending because there is no further interest rate to cut. The LM curve is horizontal, so changes in $M$ shift the LM curve right but the horizontal segment remains at $r=0$; the intersection with the IS curve stays at the same output (determined by IS alone). This is why monetary policy is 'pushing on a string'—it can tighten (raise rates) but cannot ease beyond zero.

The only way to escape a liquidity trap is to shift the IS curve right (fiscal policy) or to use unconventional monetary policy (QE, forward guidance) to lower long-term rates or increase inflation expectations (which would lower real rates).

step_by_step.step: Effective policies in a liquidity trap
step_by_step.explanation:
In a liquidity trap, the following policies are effective:

1. Fiscal policy: Government spending increases or tax cuts shift the IS curve right, increasing output without raising interest rates (since rates are stuck at zero). The fiscal multiplier is larger at the ZLB (1.5-2.0).

2. Quantitative easing (QE): The central bank buys long-term bonds, lowering long-term interest rates even when short rates are at zero. This can stimulate investment and housing.

3. Forward guidance: The central bank commits to keeping rates low for an extended period, lowering expected future short rates and thus long-term rates.

4. Negative interest rates: Some central banks (ECB, BOJ, SNB) have pushed policy rates slightly negative (e.g., -0.5%). This penalizes banks for holding excess reserves, encouraging lending. However, there is a lower bound (people can hold currency).

5. Helicopter money: Direct fiscal transfers financed by central bank money creation (monetized fiscal stimulus). This shifts both IS (fiscal) and LM (monetary) but is politically controversial.

The 2008-2015 US experience combined fiscal stimulus (ARRA) with QE and forward guidance. The 2020 pandemic response combined massive fiscal stimulus (CARES Act, ARP) with QE and forward guidance, breaking the liquidity trap (though inflation became a problem).

worked_examples.final_answer: a) Y = 1000. b) Interest rate remains 0%, output unchanged (monetary policy ineffective). c) Same; only when threshold exceeds IS output might rates rise, but not in trap. d) At ZLB, money demand is perfectly elastic; extra money is hoarded, not spent. e) Fiscal policy, QE, forward guidance, negative rates, helicopter money.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying the liquidity trap concept to analyze why monetary policy is ineffective at the zero lower bound and what policies can stimulate the economy instead.

process_flow.process: Determine real money supply (M/P) from central bank policy
process_flow.description: The central bank sets the nominal money supply M. The price level P is fixed in the short run, so real money supply is exogenous.
process_flow.mechanism: M is controlled via open market operations, QE, or reserve requirements. P is given from the fixed price assumption.

image.alt: Diagram: Central bank → M (money supply) → M/P (real money supply).
image.caption: Figure 9.3.11: Step 1 - The central bank determines the nominal money supply, which together with the fixed price level gives real money supply.

process_flow.process: Specify money demand function L(Y, r) = kY - hr
process_flow.description: Money demand depends positively on output (transactions motive) and negatively on interest rates (opportunity cost).
process_flow.mechanism: k = income sensitivity, h = interest sensitivity. These parameters are estimated from historical data.

image.alt: Diagram: Y (output) and r (interest rate) → L(Y,r) = kY - hr.
image.caption: Figure 9.3.12: Step 2 - Money demand is determined by output (positively) and interest rates (negatively).

process_flow.process: Set money market equilibrium: M/P = L(Y, r)
process_flow.description: Equate real money supply to real money demand to find combinations of Y and r that clear the money market.
process_flow.mechanism: Solve for r in terms of Y (or Y in terms of r).

image.caption: Figure 9.3.13: Step 3 - Set money market equilibrium to derive the LM curve equation.

process_flow.process: Plot the LM curve (r on y-axis, Y on x-axis)
process_flow.description: The LM curve slopes upward because higher Y increases money demand, requiring higher r to restore equilibrium.
process_flow.mechanism: Slope = k/h. Steep LM if k large or h small; flat LM if k small or h large.

image.alt: Graph: Upward-sloping LM curve; axes labeled r (vertical) and Y (horizontal).
image.caption: Figure 9.3.14: Step 4 - Plot the LM curve with the interest rate on the vertical axis and output on the horizontal axis.

process_flow.process: Analyze shocks: monetary policy shifts LM; output changes cause movement along LM
process_flow.description: Changes in M or P shift the LM curve. Changes in Y cause movements along the LM curve.
process_flow.mechanism: M ↑ → LM shifts right (lower r for each Y). Y ↑ → movement up along LM (higher r).

image.alt: Diagram: Monetary policy shift (right) and output movement (along curve).
image.caption: Figure 9.3.15: Step 5 - Analyze shocks: changes in the money supply shift the LM curve; changes in output cause movements along the curve.

image.alt: Master framework: Money supply (M) and price level (P) → real money supply; output (Y) and interest rate (r) → money demand; equilibrium gives LM curve; shifts from monetary policy; special cases (liquidity trap, classical).
image.caption: Figure 9.3.16: Complete framework for the LM curve: from money supply and demand to equilibrium to policy analysis.

summary: The LM curve (Liquidity preference-Money supply curve) shows all combinations of the real interest rate and output at which the money market is in equilibrium (real money demand equals real money supply). It slopes upward because higher output increases money demand (transactions motive), which raises the interest rate given a fixed money supply. The slope depends on the income sensitivity of money demand ($k$) and the interest sensitivity of money demand ($h$): steep LM when $k$ is large or $h$ is small; flat LM when $k$ is small or $h$ is large. The LM curve shifts when the money supply changes (monetary policy) or when the price level changes. An increase in the money supply shifts the LM curve right (lower interest rates at any output). Special cases include the liquidity trap (horizontal LM, monetary policy ineffective) and the classical case (vertical LM, fiscal policy ineffective). The LM curve is one half of the IS-LM model; together with the IS curve, it determines short-run equilibrium output and interest rates. Understanding the LM curve is essential for analyzing how monetary policy affects interest rates, the transmission of policy to the economy, and the conditions under which policy is effective (normal times) vs. ineffective (liquidity trap).
